refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable the Vehicle to complete the load→travel→dump cycle with a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, while maintaining compliance with worksite safety policies.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a KPI requirement that directly impacts customer productivity and system acceptance. Refinement clarifies that cycle time is a measurable outcome dependent on vehicle performance, operational conditions, and safety constraints. The p95 metric and duty cycle context are essential to avoid over-specification or under-specification of performance.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrumented field trials under representative duty cycle conditions, with telemetry capture of cycle time components (load, travel, dump phases). Statistical analysis of p95 across multiple runs. Comparison against CT_target baseline.
sub_frs: FR-2.1: The Vehicle shall minimize load phase duration while maintaining safe load engagement.
FR-2.2: The Vehicle shall minimize travel phase duration while maintaining safe navigation and stability.
FR-2.3: The Vehicle shall minimize dump phase duration while maintaining safe material discharge.
FR-2.4: The Safety Services shall enforce operational constraints that do not degrade cycle time below acceptable thresholds.
FR-2.5: The Teleoperation system shall enable remote operation with cycle time performance equivalent to or better than manual operation.

candidate_dps: DP-2.A: Optimize vehicle hydraulic system response time, load arm speed, and travel drivetrain acceleration to minimize phase durations independently.
DP-2.B: Implement integrated cycle time management across load, travel, and dump phases with coordinated actuation and predictive path planning.
DP-2.C: Deploy real-time telemetry feedback and adaptive control loops to dynamically adjust vehicle performance based on site conditions and safety constraints.
DP-2.D: Establish a tiered operational mode strategy (eco, standard, performance) selectable by operator to trade cycle time against fuel efficiency and wear.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-2.B: Implement integrated cycle time management across load, travel, and dump phases with coordinated actuation and predictive path planning, supported by real-time telemetry feedback (DP-2.C) and operational mode selection (DP-2.D).

potential_sources: Cycle time optimization is coupled to FR-2.4 (Safety Services constraints); safety interlocks may limit actuation speed. Mitigation: design safety constraints to be non-blocking for nominal cycle time.
Teleoperation latency (FR-2.5) may couple to cycle time if network delays exceed operator reaction time. Mitigation: establish maximum acceptable latency budget.
Site conditions (duty cycle) introduce variability; predictive path planning must adapt. Mitigation: telemetry-driven adaptive control.
coupling_check.notes: Selected DP avoids over-constraining individual phases (DP-2.A) and instead uses integrated management with feedback. This reduces coupling by allowing dynamic trade-offs. Operational modes (DP-2.D) decouple customer choice from fixed design.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: CT_target is achievable within vehicle kinematic and hydraulic limits. Risk: If target is unrealistic, no DP will satisfy FR. Mitigation: validate CT_target against vehicle physics early.

Assumption: Predictive path planning can be computed in real-time on vehicle compute platform. Risk: Computational latency may negate planning benefit. Mitigation: pre-compute common paths; use simplified models.

Assumption: Telemetry bandwidth and latency support real-time feedback loops. Risk: Network congestion or sensor delays degrade control responsiveness. Mitigation: prioritize telemetry; use local control fallback.

Assumption: Safety constraints do not mandate cycle time reductions that conflict with CT_target. Risk: Safety interlocks may force slower operation. Mitigation: co-design safety and performance requirements.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-2.B to explicitly specify the coordination strategy (e.g., sequential vs. parallel phase execution, overlap windows) and define the feedback control law (e.g., PID, MPC). Add acceptance criteria for predictive path planning accuracy and real-time compute latency.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns the mechanical, hydraulic, and electrical subsystems that execute load, travel, and dump phases. It receives Tasking commands and Telemetry requests from higher-level orchestration, and outputs Telemetry data and SafetyAlert signals. The Vehicle is the primary realization of cycle time performance.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational commands (load, travel, dump) and operational mode selection (eco, standard, performance) from NGHE Solution orchestration layer.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs real-time cycle phase timing, load mass, position, velocity, hydraulic pressures, and fuel consumption to support feedback control and KPI monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical events (e.g., stability warnings, load shift detection, emergency stop) to Safety Services for constraint enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication pathway for Tasking, Telemetry, and SafetyAlert exchange with NGHE Solution and Teleoperation modules.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestrator and would own cycle time KPI management logic, but the actual cycle time performance is realized by Vehicle subsystems. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and create tight coupling between orchestration and vehicle control. Not recommended as primary IM.

Vehicle module directly contains the hydraulic, mechanical, and electrical subsystems that execute load, travel, and dump phases. Cycle time is primarily a Vehicle performance characteristic. Integrated cycle time management (DP-2.B) maps naturally to Vehicle control architecture. High containment, low diffusion. Recommended.

Teleoperation enables remote operation but does not own vehicle actuation or cycle time performance. Operator input latency and command translation are Teleoperation concerns, but the underlying vehicle performance is Vehicle's responsibility. Not appropriate as primary IM.

Safety Services enforces operational constraints (FR-2.4) that may affect cycle time, but does not own the cycle time optimization logic itself. Safety Services would monitor and gate operations; Vehicle executes. Assigning IM to Safety Services would create confusion about performance ownership. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of cycle time performance because it contains the actuators, control systems, and kinematic/dynamic models that determine load, travel, and dump phase durations. Integrated cycle time management (DP-2.B) is realized as a Vehicle control strategy that coordinates hydraulic actuation, drivetrain response, and path planning. Real-time telemetry feedback (DP-2.C) is implemented via Vehicle sensors and onboard compute. Operational mode selection (DP-2.D) is a Vehicle parameter that modulates performance. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) allow Vehicle to receive commands and report status without diffusing cycle time ownership.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle module may become overloaded if it also owns detailed path planning and site-specific optimization. Mitigation: path planning logic can be pre-computed by NGHE Solution and downloaded to Vehicle; Vehicle executes pre-planned paths with real-time adaptation.

Risk: Telemetry bandwidth may be insufficient to support high-frequency feedback control. Mitigation: implement local control loops on Vehicle; use Telemetry for monitoring and coarse-grained adaptation.

Risk: Safety Services constraints may be imposed after Vehicle design is finalized, forcing cycle time rework. Mitigation: co-design Vehicle and Safety Services requirements; establish safety-performance trade-off matrix early.

Risk: Teleoperation latency may degrade cycle time if operator reaction time is slow. Mitigation: define acceptable latency budget; implement predictive assistance in Vehicle to reduce operator workload.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Vehicle-owned cycle time control (local, real-time) and NGHE Solution-owned cycle time orchestration (global, planning). Define the Tasking interface protocol to specify operational mode, target cycle time, and site-specific parameters. Establish a Telemetry schema that includes cycle phase timing, enabling external monitoring and adaptive control. Ensure Safety Services constraints are expressed as operational limits (e.g., max speed, max load) rather than cycle time targets, preserving Vehicle autonomy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-2.B (integrated cycle time management with coordinated actuation and predictive path planning) directly addresses FR-2 (achieve CT_target under duty cycle and site conditions). The DP is solution-neutral and does not over-specify implementation details. Coupling risks are identified and mitigated. The DP is feasible given typical heavy equipment control architectures.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle module must integrate hydraulic, mechanical, and electrical subsystems. If subsystems are not well-coordinated, cycle time optimization may diffuse across multiple sub-modules. Mitigation: establish clear Vehicle control architecture with centralized cycle time management logic.

Telemetry feedback loop requires Vehicle sensors and onboard compute. If sensor integration is poor, feedback quality degrades. Mitigation: specify sensor accuracy and latency requirements; validate sensor fusion early.

Operational mode selection (DP-2.D) must be implemented consistently across all Vehicle subsystems. If modes are implemented independently, behavior becomes unpredictable. Mitigation: define mode semantics formally; implement mode switching as atomic operation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module has high containment (0.95) for cycle time performance. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and do not diffuse responsibility. Vehicle owns the realization of DP-2.B; NGHE Solution owns orchestration and planning.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Cycle time KPI can be verified via instrumented field trials. Telemetry interface enables real-time monitoring of cycle phase timing. p95 metric can be computed from trial data. Verification is feasible and does not require invasive instrumentation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-2 is allocated to Vehicle module with clear ownership. Sub-FRs (FR-2.1 through FR-2.5) are allocated to Vehicle and Safety Services with explicit responsibility boundaries. Teleoperation's role in cycle time (FR-2.5) is clearly scoped as enabling remote operation without degrading performance.

decision.justification: FR-2 (cycle time KPI) is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs that address load, travel, dump phases, safety constraints, and teleoperation. DP-2.B is sound and maps well to Vehicle module, but the sub-FRs (FR-2.1 through FR-2.5) must be refined and decomposed further to guide detailed design. The next level will address phase-specific performance optimization, safety-performance trade-offs, and teleoperation latency budgets.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-2.1: The Vehicle shall minimize load phase duration while maintaining safe load engagement.
FR-2.2: The Vehicle shall minimize travel phase duration while maintaining safe navigation and stability.
FR-2.3: The Vehicle shall minimize dump phase duration while maintaining safe material discharge.
FR-2.4: The Safety Services shall enforce operational constraints that do not degrade cycle time below acceptable thresholds.
FR-2.5: The Teleoperation system shall enable remote operation with cycle time performance equivalent to or better than manual operation.

assumptions: CT_target is achievable within vehicle kinematic and hydraulic limits.
Predictive path planning can be computed in real-time on vehicle compute platform.
Telemetry bandwidth and latency support real-time feedback loops.
Safety constraints do not mandate cycle time reductions that conflict with CT_target.
Operator reaction time and teleoperation latency are within acceptable budgets.
Site conditions (duty cycle) are well-characterized and stable during operation.
open_risks: CT_target validation against vehicle physics not yet completed.
Computational latency of predictive path planning not yet quantified.
Network latency and bandwidth requirements not yet specified.
Safety-performance trade-off matrix not yet established.
Teleoperation latency budget not yet defined.
Vehicle control architecture (centralized vs. distributed) not yet decided.
evidence_needed: Vehicle kinematic and hydraulic performance data (max speed, acceleration, load capacity).
Duty cycle definition and site condition variability analysis.
Safety requirements and operational constraint definitions.
Computational platform specifications (processor, memory, real-time OS).
